stories.headline: China test-fires a submarine-launched ballistic missile into the Pacific, drawing protests from Australia, New Zealand and Japan
stories.summary: Beijing's navy said a strategic missile carrying a dummy warhead (an inert training payload, not a live nuclear one) was launched at 06:01 on Mon 6 July toward the high seas and "landed precisely within the designated waters." It fell inside the South Pacific Nuclear Free Zone — the region covered by the 1986 Treaty of Rarotonga, under which China in 1987 pledged not to test nuclear weapons there.

stories.headline: Russia hits Kyiv with another mass missile-and-drone barrage — at least 14 dead, the second such strike in four days
stories.summary: Overnight into Mon 6 July, Ukraine reported a combined attack of ~23 ballistic missiles, 39 cruise missiles, six Zircon hypersonic missiles (a fast, hard-to-intercept class) and 351 attack/decoy drones, with Kyiv the main target; air defences downed most drones but Zelensky said a shortage of interceptor missiles meant no ballistic missiles were stopped. It follows the 2 July strike that killed at least 21, and lands on the eve of the NATO summit — timing Zelensky tied explicitly to Putin.

stories.headline: Bern weighs making tips tax-free — and tax-law professors warn it could be gamed
stories.summary: Under current federal practice gratuities are only taxed when they form a substantial part of wages; a proposal now under discussion would exempt them outright. Legal scholars caution that a blanket carve-out invites reclassification abuse — in the limit, well-paid employees (the article's example: bankers) restyling part of their pay as untaxed "tips" — echoing the design problems that dogged the US "no tax on tips" measure. Still an early-stage proposal, not law.

stories.headline: A new German app aims to smooth cross-border "shopping tourism" for Swiss residents
stories.summary: The tool digitises the export/VAT-refund paperwork (the Ausfuhrschein that shoppers get stamped at the border to reclaim German value-added tax) that has long made day-trips to German retail a hassle for people living near the border — a small but concrete change to a routine many in Switzerland do to dodge higher domestic prices.

stories.headline: NATO leaders gather in Ankara (Tue 7–Wed 8 July); Trump expected to meet Zelensky on the sidelines
stories.summary: It is the 36th NATO summit and the second hosted by Turkey. A draft "Ankara Declaration" seen by Reuters earmarks €70bn for Ukraine, and European allies are trying to paper over friction with Washington over Iran and Greenland as the US signals a lighter alliance footprint. Trump is slated to meet Zelensky — and Syria's Ahmad al-Sharaa — on Wed 8 July.

stories.headline: Iran holds the largest day of Ayatollah Khamenei's funeral procession; his named successor Mojtaba has still not appeared in public
stories.summary: Millions filled Tehran on Mon 6 July for the main procession of the Supreme Leader, killed on 28 Feb in a US–Israeli strike; the metro logged some 7 million trips over Sat–Sun 4–5 July as crowds converged. Religious ceremonies move to Mashhad, with burial at the Imam Reza shrine set for Thu 9 July. Mojtaba Khamenei — appointed by the Assembly of Experts (the clerical body that selects Iran's leader) — remains unseen, reportedly injured and disfigured in the strike that killed his father.

stories.headline: At least 19 killed in Sri Lanka prison clashes
stories.summary: Violence broke out at the prison in Negombo on Sun 5 July and continued into Mon 6 July, leaving at least 19 dead and more than 100 wounded, authorities said.

stories.headline: The impeachment trial of Philippine Vice-President Sara Duterte opens
stories.summary: The Senate began proceedings against Duterte, daughter of former president Rodrigo Duterte — a landmark step in the country's deepening Marcos–Duterte rift.

stories.headline: The Federal Council wrapped its annual summer "course d'école" through canton Vaud on Friday (2026-07-03), hosted by Confederation President Guy Parmelin
stories.summary: The traditional two-day outing took the seven-member cabinet across La Broye, La Côte and the Chablais — deliberately beyond the Lake Geneva shoreline — with public receptions at the castles of Aubonne and Aigle and a visit to Switzerland's second-largest flour mill at Granges-près-Marnand. On a cruise down the Rhône the government was briefed on the third Rhône correction (Switzerland's largest flood-protection project, straightening and reinforcing the river's course through the Chablais plain).

stories.headline: The Swiss army says unidentified drones flew in formation over sensitive military sites — and it has no idea who is behind it
stories.summary: Army chief Benedikt Roos disclosed that drones were seen over the Jassbach barracks in the Bernese Emmental — where the army trains cyber-defence and radio-reconnaissance (signals-intelligence) specialists and runs a listening station — and over the Meiringen air base, one of Switzerland's F-35 fighter locations. Roos said the army has "no idea" who operated them and that neither the federal prosecutor nor military justice has opened a formal investigation, and he filed no complaint.

stories.headline: Trump offered to help end the war in Ukraine during a long call with Putin, according to the Kremlin
stories.summary: Kremlin aide Yuri Ushakov said the US-initiated call ran 1 hour 25 minutes and that Trump proposed his envoys Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner travel to Moscow to continue mediation; the two also discussed Iran, with Putin offering Russian help to "stabilise" the Middle East. As with prior readouts, the account is the Russian side's — no matching US statement had been published at the time of writing, so treat the framing as Moscow's.

stories.headline: Germany's AfD re-elected its co-leaders Alice Weidel (81.3%) and Tino Chrupalla (70.1%) at its Erfurt congress
stories.summary: The far-right party's 17th federal congress proceeded as planned despite tens of thousands of antifascist demonstrators attempting to blockade the venue; the new deputy chairs are Sven Tritschler, Stefan Möller and Katrin Ebner-Steiner. The comfortable margins confirm the Weidel–Chrupalla leadership is unchallenged internally as the party sits at or near the top of national polls.

stories.headline: The rear Lötschental is reachable again, a year after the Blatten rockslide buried the valley floor
stories.summary: From this weekend a Postauto (postal bus) runs several times a day from the Lauchernalp up to the Fafleralp — the tourist heart of the valley that had been all but cut off since the May 2025 landslide destroyed the village of Blatten and several hotels. The route uses a newly asphalted emergency road that passes below the Öigschtchummu glacier, which is monitored for collapses; a traffic-light warning system can halt buses if the ice moves.

stories.headline: Iran's Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei has died; a state funeral in Tehran on 4 July drew mass crowds
stories.summary: It is the structural wildcard of the week — coming after this year's war and a just-signed Iran–US framework accord that exiled Iranians are already denouncing. The succession question now hangs over the whole region.

stories.headline: Switzerland added roughly 300,000 jobs in five years — but the post-pandemic hiring boom is cooling
stories.summary: A Federal Statistical Office (BFS) review of Q4 2020–Q4 2025 finds employment at a record high: 5.4 million people in work by the population survey (+5%), or 5.5 million by the firm-based count (+7.5%). Almost all the net growth came from abroad — the foreign labour force rose 15% to about 2 million, while the number of Swiss nationals in work barely moved (+0.4% to 3.6 million); without ~120,000 naturalisations over the period the foreign share would have climbed even more (+21.8%).

stories.headline: Trump opens America's 250th-anniversary year with a "communist menace" speech at Mount Rushmore
stories.summary: On the eve of 4 July, the president praised the US military ("we won two world wars"), claimed Washington had "knocked the hell out of Iran" and "beat Venezuela in one day," and warned of a "resurgence of the communist menace" he tied to immigration — pledging that "citizens of the United States… will vanquish communism quickly." The framing is explicitly aimed at the November midterm elections (the two-yearly congressional vote that falls between presidential elections) and follows a run of progressive primary wins in New York, Colorado and Texas.

stories.headline: Stress in the private-credit market has not abated
stories.summary: Q2 redemption requests hit $15.6bn (up from $13.9bn in Q1), and Blackstone capped withdrawals from its largest fund at 5% — a slow-motion correction in a sector that ballooned since 2008 and is now touching names like Apollo and BlackRock.

stories.headline: Basel's 150-year-old textile house Schiesser is reported to be on the brink of closure
stories.summary: The traditional underwear and knitwear maker, a fixture of Basel industry for over a century and a half, faces shutting down.

stories.headline: Leading Israeli politicians are now openly floating building settlements in Gaza
stories.summary: a signal of a structural shift in post-war intentions for the territory, not just rhetoric.

stories.headline: Is One Layer Enough? Training a single transformer layer can match full-parameter RL
stories.summary: The authors run a systematic layer-by-layer study of reinforcement-learning post-training (RL — training a model from a reward signal rather than from labelled answers, the step that turns a base model into a reasoner) and find something surprising: training just one well-chosen transformer layer recovers most of — and sometimes exceeds — the gains of updating all the model's parameters. They define a "layer contribution" score and show the RL improvement concentrates in a small subset of layers, usually a single mid-stack one, while the layers near the input and output barely help.
stories.why: If RL only really touches a handful of layers, post-training could become far cheaper and — more interestingly — far more interpretable, because it localizes where in the network reasoning behaviour is being installed. It reframes RL from a mysterious global rewrite into a targeted edit. Read alongside this week's daily-brief finding that GRPO's reward design can provably collapse its gradient to rank-2 (arXiv:2606.29238), a picture emerges of RL post-training as a much lower-rank operation than its cost suggests.

stories.headline: A Hippocampus for Linear Attention: exact memory for what the recurrent state forgets
stories.summary: Linear-attention and state-space models (SSMs — recurrent alternatives to attention, like Mamba, that run in linear time) compress an entire input into a fixed-size recurrent state. That buys O(1) memory but loses precise recall once many key–value pairs compete for the same slots.
stories.why: It is a clean recipe for giving cheap sub-quadratic models the long-range exact recall they normally sacrifice, and it beats a full Transformer at the same scale — evidence that the efficiency/quality trade-off can be structural rather than fundamental.

stories.headline: Right in the Right Way: verifiable rewards and human demonstrations
stories.summary: RLVR (RL with Verifiable Rewards — training against an automatic checker: does the code pass its tests, is the math answer correct) has become the workhorse of reasoning models, but it is blind to anything un-scorable, like style or diversity, which produces reward hacking (gaming the metric without doing the task) and collapse toward a single output.
stories.why: A practical bridge between RL's checkable rewards and supervised fine-tuning's demonstrations — you get the accuracy of the checker without the tunnel vision it induces.

stories.headline: Learning the Supports for a Categorical RL Critic
stories.summary: Modern deep RL often turns value estimation — predicting expected future reward — into a classification problem over a histogram of possible returns (the "HL-Gauss" trick), which trains more stably than regressing a single number and has helped RL scale. The catch: you must hand-pick the fixed value range the histogram spans, awkward when RL's targets are non-stationary and stochastic. This paper derives an objective that learns the lower and upper bounds of that range jointly with the value function, and proves it is a tighter upper bound on the underlying error than the fixed-range version.
stories.why: It removes a finicky, task-specific knob from a technique that has quietly become important for scaling RL, and backs the fix with theory rather than just benchmarks.

stories.headline: Maven: evidence-state rewards for long-context reasoning
stories.summary: When a model reasons over a very long document, standard RL rewards only the final answer — giving no feedback on whether an intermediate step actually improved the evidence the model is holding. Maven gives the model an editable "evidence memory" and defines an answer-conditioned value over its state, then rewards each action by its marginal contribution: add actions are credited by how much they improve answer support, link actions by evidence synergy, drop actions by how much removing a distractor helps.
stories.why: It reframes long-context reasoning as stateful evidence navigation with dense, per-action credit — a promising alternative to the sparse "was the final answer right?" signal that dominates RL today.

stories.headline: WorldSample: closed-loop real-robot RL with a grounded world model
stories.summary: Reinforcement learning on physical robots is throttled by how expensive real trials are — each rollout gives one action–outcome path and takes real seconds of hardware time. WorldSample closes a real-to-synthetic loop: a handful of real rollouts ground a learned world model (a simulator that predicts the next camera image and reward from an action), which then generates many synthetic transitions.
stories.why: Trial-and-error learning has been largely impractical on real robots; generating grounded synthetic experience without the usual hallucination collapse is a concrete step toward changing that.

stories.headline: Conditional Co-Ablation: recovering hidden self-repair backups in transformer circuits
stories.summary: Mechanistic interpretability — reverse-engineering the internal "circuits" a model uses — usually scores each component by ablating it (zeroing it out) and measuring the damage. But transformers "self-repair": knock out a primary attention head and a dormant backup silently takes over, so both look unimportant in isolation. CoAx recasts this as a recovery task, with a label-free score for how much each remaining unit's importance grows once a primary set is removed — exposing the second-order interaction single-unit scores miss.
stories.why: It shows "which components matter" is not a property you can read off one unit at a time — a correction that ripples into attribution, capability removal, and pruning wherever redundancy hides the real circuitry.

stories.headline: Lie-detector oversight of LLMs scales the right way: undetected deception falls from 34% to 14% as models grow from 1B to 405B parameters
stories.summary: The idea (SOLiD — Scalable Oversight via Lie Detectors) is to train a cheap classifier that reads a model's internal activations and flags responses that look deceptive, then route only those flagged responses to expensive human reviewers during preference fine-tuning (the RLHF-style step where human ratings shape the model's behaviour). This paper scales that setup up to 405-billion-parameter models and tests it in more realistic preference-learning conditions.
stories.why: the encouraging result is that oversight gets easier, not harder, as models grow — undetected deception dropped to 14% at 405B, and the authors could remove the human labelers from the fine-tuning loop entirely without a statistically significant rise in deception. The honest caveat they flag: the detector is sensitive to distribution shift between the data it was trained on and the preference data, which can push its false-positive rate around — so this is a promising oversight lever, not a solved problem.

stories.headline: Ticino freezes ~50M CHF in tax rebates owed to Italy
stories.summary: On Tuesday 30 June the Ticino cantonal government partially suspended the rétrocessions fiscales (the share of frontier-worker withholding tax that Switzerland pays back to Italian border municipalities under the 1974 cross-border accord), blocking some 50 million francs "as a precaution, pending clarifications," said government president Claudio Zali (Lega dei Ticinesi). The move follows weeks of tension with Lombardy and Bern over a health-tax dispute affecting cross-border workers; Bern says it regrets the decision and wants a negotiated solution. Italy protested.

stories.headline: A character-level jailbreak exploits how tokenizers chop up safety words, flipping refusals on 80–100% of blocked prompts
stories.summary: BPE (byte-pair encoding — the standard scheme that splits text into sub-word "tokens") fragments a safety-critical word into pieces when it is lightly perturbed, and the authors show the three public alignment datasets they surveyed contain no such deliberately fragmented examples — so the safety training never saw this failure mode.
stories.why: it names a concrete, structural reason character-level attacks keep working — the tokenizer, not the model's "intent" — and points at a fixable gap: put fragmented inputs into alignment data. The prompts stay human-readable throughout, which is what makes the attack awkward to filter.

stories.headline: "NightVision" recovers an LLM's hidden dimension, depth, and parameter count through today's locked-down single-logit APIs
stories.summary: Earlier attacks recovered architectural details (like the width of a model's feed-forward layers) when APIs exposed top-k logits or a logit-bias knob; providers responded by exposing only the single chosen token per step. This work shows that even under those restrictive APIs, an attacker can still estimate the hidden dimension, the number of layers (depth), and the total parameter count.
stories.why: commercial labs treat architecture as a trade secret, and this says the current API lockdown isn't enough to hide it — relevant both to anyone relying on that secrecy and to the broader question of how much a model's internals leak through its outputs.

stories.headline: Kara compresses the KV cache with a sliding window to speed up long-reasoning serving
stories.summary: Reasoning models emit long chains of thought, and each generated token's key/value vectors pile up in the KV cache (the running memory of attention), which balloons memory use and slows decoding. Existing compression drops "unimportant" cache entries, but the authors argue the common threshold-triggered policies can barely help throughput — or even hurt it — and can wipe out whole blocks of the sequence, losing information. Kara instead compresses over a sliding window with a more graceful retention rule.
stories.why: long-CoT (chain-of-thought) serving is where a lot of today's inference cost sits, so a compression method that improves throughput without the accuracy cliffs of prior schemes is directly useful for anyone self-hosting reasoning models.

stories.headline: Vaud: Nuria Gorrite will not seek re-election in 2027
stories.summary: After 15 years in the cantonal executive, the Socialist (PS) minister — head of the culture, infrastructure and HR department, and former Morges mayor — announced she will not run for a fourth term at the February 2027 cantonal election, respecting her party's internal term limits. She said the decision is unrelated to the "affaire Dittli" roiling Vaud politics and left her federal ambitions open. Vaud's three PLR (centre-right) ministers — Luisier, Borloz and Moret — said Thursday they will all stand again.

stories.headline: SNB says Swiss banks — including UBS — are well capitalised; UBS calls the report "misleading."
stories.summary: The Swiss National Bank's 2026 Financial Stability Report (released 2 July) concluded the sector is robust and that UBS can meet the Federal Council's proposed 2030 capital rules. Bern's 22 April proposal to make banks fully back foreign subsidiaries with capital would require UBS to add roughly $20bn of CET1 (common equity tier 1 — the highest-quality, first-loss capital), but the SNB notes UBS already exceeds current 2030 requirements by about $13bn and could comply while still paying dividends.

stories.headline: Khamenei's state funeral opens in Tehran; 100+ countries send delegations
stories.summary: Iran began a seven-day state funeral (3–10 July) for Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, killed aged 86 on 28 February in a joint US–Israeli airstrike on his compound on the first day of the US–Israel war; the burial, first set for March, was delayed by the war. Representatives from more than 100 countries are attending the opening in Tehran, including Russia's Dmitry Medvedev, Pakistani PM Shehbaz Sharif, the Taliban government's foreign minister Amir Khan Muttaqi, senior Chinese lawmaker He Wei, and India's deputy foreign minister.

stories.headline: The execution substrate is a hidden tax on coding-agent RL: up to 110× variation in cold-start latency across four backends
stories.summary: Training a coding agent with reinforcement learning means running huge numbers of interactive software "rollouts" — spinning up an environment, letting the agent edit and run code, scoring the result. The infrastructure that hosts those rollouts is usually treated as a background detail. The authors benchmark four substrates — single containers, hosted sandboxes, Kubernetes-orchestrated containers, and cloud VMs — and find up to 110× spread in cold-start latency and a 1.8× spread in projected worker-hours for one million 150-step trajectories.
stories.why: small per-rollout savings compound across millions of rollouts, so the paper's argument is that the execution substrate should be optimized as part of the training system, not bolted on — a practical lever for the cost and speed of RL post-training that most teams currently ignore.

stories.headline: Procedural Memory Distillation reuses cross-episode RL signals that per-episode updates throw away
stories.summary: In RLVR (reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards — score each attempt with an automatic checker rather than a human), the policy is updated from a single episode-level pass/fail signal, and the richer detail inside each rollout is discarded. But across episodes the model keeps meeting related problems and generating signals about which strategies pass, which failure modes persist, and which patterns recur. PMD captures that cross-episode information as reusable "procedural memory" and distills it back into the model's weights during training.
stories.why: it targets a real inefficiency in how verifier-based RL learns — treating each attempt as isolated — and offers a way to let a model accumulate reusable skill rather than relearning the same lessons every epoch.

stories.headline: "Token Geometry": a kilobyte-scale optimizer for embedding tables that undercuts Adam's memory
stories.summary: A language model's embedding table and output ("LM-head") layer are the read/write interface between discrete tokens and the continuous internal computation, and the author argues their gradient geometry differs from the dense hidden weights — so they deserve a different optimizer. The proposed method, Ember, tracks optimizer state in O(V + D) memory (V = vocabulary size, D = model dimension) instead of Adam's O(2VD), i.e. kilobytes rather than the usual large per-parameter buffers, and avoids having to shard those states.
stories.why: embedding/LM-head optimizer state is a surprisingly heavy slice of training memory for large-vocabulary models, so a method that shrinks it to kilobytes while improving the accuracy-vs-cost trade-off across pretraining, supervised fine-tuning, and RL is a concrete efficiency win. — a single-author claim awaiting independent replication.

stories.headline: A "three-term" scaling law splits training data into steps and batch size — and recovers the optimal batch size from fewer runs
stories.summary: Standard scaling laws (empirical formulas predicting loss from model size and total training data) lump all the data together. This work explicitly factors the data term into training steps × batch size, fits the resulting law across many runs, and shows it correctly recovers how the optimal batch size should scale — and, usefully, can be fit robustly from far fewer training runs because it can exploit runs that used a suboptimal batch size.
stories.why: batch size is a costly hyperparameter to tune by brute force; a law that predicts the right value (and matches prior "critical batch size" findings) means less compute wasted on sweeps before a big run.

stories.headline: CreativityNeuro steers a model's weights to boost divergent thinking and blunt "mode collapse"
stories.summary: LLMs tend to give near-identical answers to open-ended prompts — the "artificial hivemind" effect, a form of mode collapse where the model keeps returning to the same few outputs. CreativityNeuro is a data-free method (no extra training data needed) that steers the weights by contrasting more- and less-creative behaviours. On the Divergent Association Task (a vocabulary-based creativity test) it improved scores by up to 14 human-percentile points, and a large human study (N=720) on the Alternative Uses Test rated its outputs higher on originality, surprise, and creativity.
stories.why: it's a lightweight, training-data-free knob for the diversity of a model's outputs, backed by an unusually large human evaluation rather than an automated proxy — relevant wherever repetitive, samey generations are the failure mode.

stories.headline: Anthropic ships Claude Sonnet 5, its most agentic Sonnet yet, at performance close to Opus 4.8 but lower cost
stories.summary: Anthropic positions Sonnet 5 as a substantial jump over Sonnet 4.6 on agentic tasks — planning, tool use (browsers and terminals), coding, and knowledge work — running autonomously at a level that recently required larger, pricier models; it reports performance close to Opus 4.8 at a lower price point. Anthropic also says its safety assessments found a lower overall rate of undesirable behaviours than Sonnet 4.6 and a "much lower" ability to perform cyber-offensive tasks; the fuller evaluation set is in the Sonnet 5 System Card.

stories.headline: Federal social-insurance projections turn grim: disability fund (IV/AI) reserves could run dry by 2031
stories.summary: The Federal Social Insurance Office (OFAS/BSV) published updated financial projections Thursday warning that the IV — Switzerland's disability insurance — could see its reserves disappear as early as 2031, with SRF reporting the scheme heading toward an 800-million-franc annual deficit. The old-age pension (AVS, the state retirement pension, AHV in German) is under parallel pressure and needs durable financing for the newly approved 13th monthly payment. This is the numbers behind a debate that directly shapes future payroll deductions and pension levels for anyone working in Switzerland.

stories.headline: EFTA and Vietnam sign a comprehensive free-trade agreement
stories.summary: Switzerland, Iceland, Liechtenstein and Norway — the EFTA bloc (European Free Trade Association, the four non-EU Western European states) — concluded a deal that abolishes all Swiss tariffs on industrial goods and fish from Vietnam; Vietnam will phase out duties on watches, machinery, chemicals, cheese and chocolate over up to 11 years. Economics minister Guy Parmelin welcomed the deal, which lands as a rare win in a difficult trade week — parliament's lower house rejected the Mercosur pact last week and a US tariff framework expires end-July.

stories.headline: Vaud: three PLR cantonal ministers signal 2027 re-election bids
stories.summary: Christelle Luisier, Isabelle Moret and Frédéric Borloz — the three PLR (centre-right liberal-radical party) members of the Vaud cantonal government (Conseil d'État) — confirmed they intend to stand again in the 2027 cantonal elections, an early marker for how the seven-seat executive's balance may hold.

stories.headline: Violent thunderstorms swept the country overnight into Wednesday, closing roads and grounding flights
stories.summary: SRF Meteo logged more than 25,000 lightning strikes. In Valais, a mudslide (Murgang — a debris flow of mud, water and rock) blocked the cantonal road between Les Évouettes and Le Bouveret at the eastern end of Lake Geneva twice — once after midnight, once around midday — dumping thousands of cubic metres of debris. Debris flows also shut the Sustenpass and Klausen roads in Uri. In Bern, Bernmobil halted tram service for ~40 minutes on Wednesday morning; police fielded a dozen reports of fallen trees and flooded buildings across the Bernese Oberland.

stories.headline: Graphene hosts a whole family of superconductors that magnetic fields strengthen
stories.summary: In almost every known superconductor, a magnetic field is the enemy: it pries apart the paired electrons (Cooper pairs — the bound electron duos that carry lossless current) and kills the state above a ceiling called the Pauli limit. This paper reports the opposite in rhombohedral pentalayer graphene (five carbon sheets stacked in a particular ABC registry): a family of distinct superconducting phases that are either enhanced or outright created by an in-plane magnetic field, surviving to 8.5 tesla — tens of times past the Pauli limit.
stories.why: A single, gate-tunable, all-carbon platform where you can switch between superconducting flavors — and where magnetic field helps instead of hurts — is an unusually clean laboratory for the physics behind exotic superconductivity, and hints at pairing that could tolerate conditions that destroy ordinary superconductors.

stories.headline: A batch of new federal rules took effect today, 1 July
stories.summary: several with direct household impact. Physical punishment and "degrading treatment" of children are now explicitly banned in civil law (cantons must offer accessible family counselling). Several traffic offences shift from criminal charges to fixed administrative fines (e.g. passing on the right on the motorway → CHF 200; wrong roundabout use → CHF 100). Incoming calls from abroad that display a Swiss number must now be specially marked so carriers can identify and block spoofed numbers (anti-fraud measure).

stories.headline: The Society of St Pius X (SSPX) — the traditionalist Catholic fraternity headquartered in Ecône, Valais — consecrated four new bishops on Wednesday over the Vatican's objections, opening what looks like a fresh rupture with Rome
stories.summary: Superior General Abbé Davide Pagliarani framed the consecrations as securing the fraternity's survival ("we cannot choose between the faith and the Church"). Under Church law, unauthorised episcopal consecrations trigger automatic excommunication — the same mechanism that split the SSPX from Rome in 1988 — and canonical exclusion now looks imminent despite protests from Pope Leo XIV. The Diocese of Sion voiced its "profound sadness."

stories.headline: An old rotation sensor gets un-stuck: a laser gyroscope beats the lock-in limit
stories.summary: A ring laser gyroscope senses rotation via the Sagnac effect — two laser beams circling the same ring in opposite directions pick up a tiny frequency difference when the ring turns. The catch is "lock-in": at slow rotation the two beams synch up and the device goes blind in a dead zone, which is why aircraft-grade units need mechanical dithering and careful engineering to work around it.
stories.why: Sensitive near-zero-rate rotation sensing without the usual mechanical crutches points toward smaller, cheaper, drift-free gyroscopes for navigation where GPS can't reach — and revives a decades-old sensor concept by solving its defining flaw.

stories.headline: Three separate labs, one shared quantum state: a step toward a real quantum network
stories.summary: Entangling two matter qubits over a distance is now routine on several platforms; stretching that to three or more independent nodes has stayed "formidably challenging," in the authors' words, because the light–matter interfaces that convert a stored qubit into a flying photon are inefficient and losses compound with every added node.
stories.why: A quantum network's power comes from linking many nodes, not two; demonstrating efficient, storable three-node entanglement is the kind of scaling step that separates a lab curiosity from the beginnings of a modular quantum computer or a long-distance quantum-secure link.

stories.headline: The overlooked molecule that helps seed marine clouds — and is missing from climate models
stories.summary: New atmospheric particles form when trace gases cluster into nuclei that then grow — the seeds that eventually become cloud droplets. Sulfuric acid usually gets the credit; this study, using CERN's CLOUD chamber (a giant sealed vessel that recreates atmospheric nucleation under controlled conditions), shows that methanesulfonic acid (MSA — a sulfur compound made when marine plankton emissions break down) is a serious co-driver.
stories.why: Clouds are one of the largest uncertainties in climate projections, and how many seeds existed in the pre-industrial atmosphere sets the baseline against which human-caused changes are measured. A biogenic seed source that current climate models ignore is exactly the kind of gap that shifts those estimates.

stories.headline: A vaccine finally coaxes primates into making broadly neutralizing HIV antibodies
stories.summary: HIV's defense is variability: its surface envelope mutates so fast that antibodies against one strain miss the next. The decades-long dream has been a vaccine that trains the immune system to make broadly neutralizing antibodies (bnAbs — antibodies that hit conserved sites shared across strains). Earlier work only managed to wake up the rare naive B cells that could one day become bnAb-makers (the "germline-targeting" priming step).
stories.why: Going from "primed precursors" to actual cross-neutralizing antibodies in an outbred primate is the proof-of-concept the field has chased for years, and the strongest signal yet that a staged bnAb-eliciting HIV vaccine could work in humans.

stories.headline: Researchers assemble a cell from non-living parts and watch it grow and divide
stories.summary: Bottom-up synthetic biology tries to build a living cell by packing non-living molecules into a membrane rather than editing an existing organism. This group reports "SpudCell": a fatty-membrane droplet loaded with a stripped-down genome (far smaller than any bacterium's — on the order of a few dozen genes) plus the machinery to read it, which took up nutrients, replicated its DNA and physically divided into daughter cells for several generations — and showed a faint hint of selection, since larger cells left more offspring.
stories.why: If it holds up, combining growth, DNA replication, division and a whiff of heredity in a scratch-built cell is a genuine landmark for understanding what minimally counts as "alive" — but until the paper and a replication land, treat it as a striking claim, not a settled result.

stories.headline: 1,167 hours of listening pin down where a hyperactive fast radio burst goes silent
stories.summary: Fast radio bursts (FRBs) are millisecond flashes of radio energy from cosmological distances whose engines are still debated; a small number "repeat," letting astronomers study them in depth. This campaign trained the Allen Telescope Array on the hyperactive repeater FRB 20240114A for 1,167 hours spanning ~900 MHz to 7.6 GHz simultaneously — an unusually wide instantaneous band. It caught 97 bursts between ~900 MHz and ~5 GHz, including a strong burst episode in S-band (~2–4 GHz), but none in the highest-frequency tunings above ~5 GHz.
stories.why: Establishing a frequency ceiling for a prolific repeater is the kind of hard observational boundary that theories of what powers FRBs — magnetars, shocks, or something else — have to fit, narrowing a field still rich in competing models.

stories.headline: GRPO's reward design provably collapses its gradient to rank-2
stories.summary: Derives GRPO (Group Relative Policy Optimization — the critic-free RL recipe behind DeepSeek-R1-style training, which replaces a learned value network with the average reward of a group of sampled answers) from the policy-gradient theorem and exposes a built-in flaw: when the reward is given only for the final answer, every token in a generated answer gets the same credit, so the learning signal collapses to a single number per answer.
stories.why: it gives a precise account of when GRPO's appealing simplicity stops working, which is exactly the recipe most open RL pipelines now copy.

stories.headline: Left and unions launch a referendum against parliament's curb on cantonal minimum wages
stories.summary: The Socialists, Greens and the unions (Travail.Suisse, Unia) launched a referendum on Tuesday after the Federal Assembly voted to subordinate cantonal and communal minimum wages to extended collective bargaining agreements (CCTs — sector-wide pay deals between employers and unions) — meaning the CCT rate would prevail where one exists. PS co-president Cédric Wermuth called the reform "a bosses' coup d'état.

stories.headline: A lot of RL stabilization may be improving the wrong policy
stories.summary: Tackles the "training–inference mismatch" that makes LLM RL unstable: a model is generated with one engine (optimized for speed) but updated with another (optimized for precision), so the same answer gets slightly different probabilities on each side — a hidden off-policy gap that quietly poisons training. The paper's argument is that prior fixes aim at the wrong target: an update that improves the training policy doesn't necessarily improve the inference policy, which is the one you actually deploy. It reframes the real objective as monotonically improving the inference policy.
stories.why: if correct, a lot of RL-stabilization effort has been optimizing the wrong quantity.

stories.headline: EAPO reuses past experience to cut reasoning-RL's sample cost
stories.summary: EAPO (Experience-Augmented Policy Optimization) attacks the sample-cost of RLVR (Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards — RL where correctness is checked by a verifier, e.g. did the math answer match) for reasoning. Instead of reusing past successful answers as fixed reasoning traces (which go stale as the policy changes), it treats a prior RL-optimized policy as an action-level experience prior and injects it only at critical decision points during generation. Tested on Qwen-2.5-Math-7B and Qwen-3-8B.
stories.why: reasoning RL is expensive because it samples from scratch; reusing experience adaptively rather than verbatim is a plausible efficiency lever.

stories.headline: A broad alliance launches a referendum against building new nuclear plants
stories.summary: Following parliament's decision to clear the legal path for new reactors, a wide coalition launched a referendum and must gather the 50,000 signatures by 8 October; a national vote would follow no earlier than February 2027. The fight reopens the post-Fukushima nuclear question — Switzerland had legislated a phase-out (no new plants) in 2017 — and pits new-build advocates against renewables-first critics who, like Valais MP Christophe Clivaz, call the nuclear revival "an ideological decision that slows renewables."

stories.headline: From 1 July, Switzerland may export less steel duty-free to the EU
stories.summary: Bern and Brussels agreed new quotas under the EU's tightened steel safeguard regime taking effect 1 July: the bloc cuts total tariff-free import volumes by 47% to 18.3 million tonnes a year and raises the above-quota duty from 25% to 50%. Switzerland and eleven other partner states see their duty-free allowances fall ~33%. Brussels says it is responding to global overcapacity — China is named as the main culprit, with steel diverted to Europe after US trade barriers; the framework was negotiated under WTO rules in Geneva.

stories.headline: Swiss Space Systems founder Pascal Jaussi sentenced to 5½ years for fraud
stories.summary: Fribourg's economic criminal court (Tribunal pénal économique) on Monday convicted the founder of the failed spaceflight start-up S3 — once hyped as a low-cost satellite-launch venture — of fraud (escroquerie) among other charges, handing down a substantial custodial term; written reasons are to follow.

stories.headline: Parliament's nuclear-build-ban repeal is headed for the ballot box
stories.summary: The "Blackout stoppen" committee has conditionally withdrawn its initiative, judging that Parliament's indirect counter-proposal — which lifts the new-nuclear-construction ban voters approved in 2017 — already meets its aim. The Greens, Green-Liberals and Greenpeace have announced a referendum against that repeal, making a national vote on whether Switzerland may build new reactors likely.

stories.headline: ETH Zurich/PSI study: net-zero by 2050 is reachable without new reactors
stories.summary: A joint study by ETH Zurich (the federal technical university) and the Paul Scherrer Institute concludes Switzerland can hit its 2050 net-zero-emissions target without building new nuclear plants — and that any new plant would only be cost-competitive with state financial support. The finding lands squarely in the live debate above.

stories.headline: The US–Iran ceasefire frayed back into open strikes
stories.summary: After an attack on a vessel in the Strait of Hormuz, the US struck Iran and the two sides traded blame, putting the fragile truce — brokered in part through Swiss good offices at Bürgenstock earlier this month — at risk of collapse.

stories.headline: Trump threatened Europe with new tariffs over digital-services taxes
stories.summary: reviving a transatlantic trade fight that bears directly on Switzerland and the EU, where several digital-tax plans are live.

stories.headline: A record heatwave forced Switzerland to throttle the Beznau nuclear plant
stories.summary: , because the Aare's water ran too warm to cool the reactor; Basel set a new June temperature record before forecasters promised relief in the coming week.

stories.headline: Liquid AI ships LFM2.5-230M, a browser-sized multilingual model
stories.summary: Liquid's LFM2 architecture (a hybrid that mixes short convolutions with attention instead

stories.headline: OpenAI and Broadcom unveil "Jalapeño," OpenAI's first custom inference chip — a purpose-built ASIC, not a repurposed GPU
stories.summary: OpenAI announced its first "Intelligence Processor," an accelerator co-designed with Broadcom (NASDAQ: AVGO) and aimed specifically at inference — running already-trained models to serve answers, as opposed to training them. The pitch is architectural: an ASIC (application-specific integrated circuit — a chip hard-wired for one job rather than general-purpose like a GPU) tuned to the actual bottlenecks of large-language-model serving — chiefly data movement (shuttling the model's weights and the running context in and out of memory, which dominates inference cost more than raw arithmetic…

stories.headline: Alibaba's Qwen team open-weights "Qwen-AgentWorld" — language models that simulate environments for agents, under Apache 2.0
stories.summary: Two models shipped: Qwen-AgentWorld-35B-A3B and a larger 397B-A17B (the A figure is the active parameter count per token — these are Mixture-of-Experts models, MoE, where only a small slice of the total weights fires for any given token, so a 397B model runs at roughly the cost of a 17B one). The idea is a language world model: instead of acting in an environment, the model predicts what the environment would do next given an action — a learned simulator of state transitions, built from 10M+ real interaction trajectories across 7 task domains.

stories.headline: Today was light on major weight drops
stories.summary: Hugging Face's "created today" list was dominated by fine-tunes, quantizations, and test repos — no frontier-tier base model or notable new open-weight release from a major lab. Worth noting only because the prior fortnight was unusually dense (GLM-5.2, MiniMax M3, Baidu Unlimited-OCR, Mistral OCR 4); the cadence cooled today. — huggingface.co/models?sort=created
